note:
这笔资金来自一系列战略投资者，包括韩国SK电信(SK Telecom)、加拿大保险集团工业联盟(Industrial Alliance)、富士康(Foxconn)和Yozma Korea。在分两批交付5100万美元之后，这笔资金迅速到位，最近一次是在6月份来自战略投资者SK Telecom，该公司正在韩国建设一家工厂，生产Nanox硬件。
该公司没有披露其估值，但在6月份，估值为6亿美元，据我们所知，这很可能是该公司上市前的最后一次融资，尽管后者的时间表尚未确定(当然，在创业融资领域，永远不会说永远不会)。
Nanox创始人兼首席执行官兰·波利亚金在接受采访时表示，如今，这家初创公司的大部分收入来自许可交易：它向富士康、SK和富士(另一家投资者)等制造商提供知识产权，以制造基于其概念的设备，并计划在未来几年向市场推出约1.5万台扫描仪。
长期计划是在所有安装了机器的市场获得监管部门的批准，开始推出其业务的服务端，为通过硬件方式获取的成像提供洞察力-这一过程正在进行中，但由于冠状病毒的传播(以及随后许多过程的放缓，如获得监管部门的批准和批准)，这一过程已被推迟。
Nanox业务的这一服务方面特别令人感兴趣，因为它突显了医疗服务提供方式和未来提供方式的重大转变。
也就是说，更强大的通信网络的崛起，更好的成像技术，以及雇佣人员和保持昂贵设备更新的高昂开销，导致了一波交易浪潮，医院和其他公司将一些分析工作从现场实验室外包到远程设施。这导致了寻求利用由此产生的新机遇的企业激增。
“电信运营商正在围绕如何销售5G寻找机会，”SK电信总裁金一龙(Ilong Kim)在6月份的一次采访中表示。“现在你可以想象一下，这种大小的扫描仪用在救护车上，使用的是5G数据。它改变了整个行业的游戏规则。“。
这意味着，即使在等待监管部门批准的同时，Nanox已经在与客户签约使用这项服务-这是时代和市场需求的标志。最近，它与美国放射学公司(USA Radiology)签署了一项协议，后者将在美国各地以及其他15个国家使用这项技术开展扫描即服务业务。
当然，Nanox从这些交易中获得了两方面的好处：它不仅获得了使用这些服务的技术许可，而且还获得了与正在建造的使用这些服务的硬件相关的许可费。
如前所述，Nanox系统基于专有的数字X射线技术，这是成像领域的一个相对较新的领域，依靠数字扫描而不是X射线板来捕获和处理图像。Nanox的旗舰ARC硬件为70公斤，而普通CT扫描仪为2000公斤，生产成本约为10,000美元，而CT扫描仪为100-300万美元。
除了更小(因此更便宜)的机器，大部分图像处理都是在云中完成的，根据首席执行官兼创始人奥兰·波利亚金的说法，Nanox系统可以在极小的零点几秒内制作出图像，与现有方法相比，这使得它们在辐射暴露方面要安全得多。这使得拥有这些机器变得更容易，成本也更低，以及用它们进行定期扫描，不仅覆盖身体的一小部分，而且覆盖整个身体，这也为获得更多洞察力打开了大门。
纳诺克斯的主张目前特别令人信服。正如我们之前指出的，成像最近经常出现在新闻中，因为到目前为止，就如何影响患者的肺部和其他器官而言，成像是检测患者或潜在患者新冠肺炎进展最准确的方法之一。但另一方面，全球健康大流行，以及让人们身体上远离彼此的努力，也表明医疗界对使远程诊断患者变得更容易的服务的巨大需求，这使得Nanox及其方法处于所有医药和医疗保健似乎正在演变的核心。
前提是它获得了必要的批准，并将其业务带到了预期的下一个水平。
波利亚金在一份声明中说：“说我们的目标是改变世界很容易。”“这类语句的主要挑战始终是执行。我们有一个大胆的愿景，即通过早期发现来帮助根除癌症和其他疾病。我们正在积极为部署全球医学影像服务基础设施而努力，该基础设施可能会将这一梦想变成现实。“
note_en:
The money is coming from a range of strategic investors that include SK Telecom, Industrial Alliance (the Canadian insurance group), Foxconn and Yozma Korea, and it has arrived swiftly on the heels of $51 million delivered in two tranches, the  most recent being in June from strategic investor SK Telecom, which is building a factory to manufacture Nanox hardware in South Korea.
 The company is not disclosing its valuation but in June, it was $600 million, and from what we understand this is likely to be the company’s last fundraise before it goes public, although a timeline for the latter has not been set (and never say never in the world of startup funding, of course).
  Ran Poliakine, Nanox’s founder and CEO, said in an interview that today the startup makes the majority of its revenues from licensing deals: it provides IP to manufacturers like Foxconn, SK and Fuji (another investor) to build devices based on its concepts, and the plan is to have some 15,000 scanners out in the market over the next several years.
 The plan longer term is to get regulatory approval across all markets where the machines are in place to start to roll out the services side of its business, providing insights into the imaging that is sourced by way of the hardware — a process that is in play but has been delayed due to the spread of the coronavirus (and the subsequent slowdown of many processes, such as getting regulatory approvals and clearance).
 This services aspect of Nanox’s business is particularly interesting, as it underscores a major shift in how medical services are delivered and will be delivered in the future.
 Namely, the rise of more powerful communications networks, and better technology for imaging, and the high overhead of employing people and keeping costly equipment up to date, has led to a wave of deals where hospitals and others are outsourcing some of the analytics work away from on-site labs to remote facilities. And that has led to a surge of businesses looking to tap into the new opportunities arising out of that.
 “Telecoms carriers are looking for opportunities around how to sell 5G,” said Ilung Kim, SK Telecom’s president, in an interview in June. “Now you can imagine a scanner of this size being used in an ambulance, using 5G data. It’s a game changer for the industry.”
 This means that, even as it is waiting for regulatory clearance, Nanox is already signing on customers for this service — a sign of the times and the demand in the market. Most recently, it inked a deal with  USA Radiology, which will be using the tech for a scan-as-a-service business across the US as well as in 15 other countries.
 Nanox, of course, benefits on two sides with those deals: not only is it licensing its tech for the services, but it’s getting licensing fees connected to the hardware that’s being built to use them.
 As we have described before, the Nanox system is based around proprietary digital X-ray technology, a relatively new area in imaging that relies on digital scans rather than X-ray plates to capture and process images. Nanox’s flagship ARC hardware comes in at 70 kg compared to 2,000 kg for the average CT scanner, and production costs are around $10,000 compared to $1-3 million for the CT scanner.
 In addition to being smaller (and thus cheaper) machines with much of the processing of images done in the cloud, the Nanox system, according to CEO and founder Ran Poliakine, can make its images in a tiny fraction of a second, making them significantly safer in terms of radiation exposure compared to existing methods. This makes it easier and cheaper to own the machines, as well as to take regular scans with them, covering not just one small area of the body but the full length of it, which opens the door also to gaining more insights.
 Nanox’s proposition is particularly compelling at the moment. As we’ve pointed out before, imaging has been in the news a lot of late because it has so far been one of the most accurate methods for detecting the progress of COVID-19 in patients or would-be patients in terms of how it is affecting patients’ lungs and other organs. But on the other hand, the global health pandemic, and the push to keep people physically away from each other, has also pointed to a big need in the healthcare community for services that make it easier to diagnose patients remotely, putting Nanox and its approach right at the heart of how all of medicine and healthcare seem to be evolving.
 That’s provided that it gets the necessary approvals and takes its business to the intended next level.
 “It is easy to say that we are aiming to change the world,” Poliakine said in a statement. “The main challenge with such statements is always the execution. We have a bold vision of helping to eradicate cancer and other disease by means of early detection. We are actively working for the deployment of a global medical imaging service infrastructure that may turn this dream into reality.”
title: Nanox筹集5900万美元用于低成本、缩小尺寸的扫描技术，以取代X射线
title_en: Nanox raises $59M for low-cost, downsized scanning tech to replace X-rays

note:
19世纪90年代，巴黎一位意志坚定的年轻女子以她在放射性方面的革命性发现让科学界大放异彩，这是一部关于玛丽·居里生平的电影，根据劳伦·雷德尼斯(Lauren Redniss)2010年的漫画小说“放射性：玛丽与皮埃尔·居里、爱情与失败的故事”(Radiative：Marie&Amp；Pierre Curie)改编，由劳伦·雷德尼斯(Lauren Redniss)执导。导演Marjane Satrapi的电影部分是认真的传记片，部分是艺术片，由Rosamund Pike(“消失的女孩”)饰演的玛丽·居里的明亮、激烈和引人入胜的表演提升了这部电影的档次。
萨特拉皮最出名的可能是她强大的自传体回忆录《波斯波利斯》，以图画小说的形式描绘了她在伊斯兰革命期间在伊朗的童年，后来她将其改编成了一部动画片。因此，她钦佩关于玛丽·居里的漫画小说也就不足为奇了。尽管如此，Satrapi在一次采访中承认，她最初不愿接受这个项目。我在想，见鬼，你为什么要再拍一部关于玛丽·居里的剧本？她告诉WWD，现在已经有四个了。最终，她痴迷于拍摄这部电影，她认为这部电影既是关于玛丽·居里发现的后果，也是关于她的生活和科学。
虽然萨特拉皮使用杰克·索恩的剧本草稿作为她电影的基础，但她也挖掘了玛丽的日记和信件作为素材，并会见了居里夫妇的孙女、核物理学家海莱娜·朗之万-若里奥(Hélène Langevin-Joliot)，希望更多地了解这位独特的女人。萨特拉皮告诉WWD，我真的试着把她描绘成一个人。她并不完美，也不是每件事都做对--但谁做得对呢？(美国物理研究所长期以来一直在网上维护一个详细的展览，为那些热衷于深入研究细节的人提供玛丽·居里和放射性科学。--编者注：玛丽·居里[Marie Curie]和“放射性科学”(Science Of Raditivity)是为那些热衷于深入研究细节的人准备的。)(美国物理研究所长期以来一直在维护一个详细的在线展览，玛丽·居里和放射性科学)。我还强烈推荐苏珊·奎恩(Susan Quinn)1996年出版的传记“玛丽·居里：一生”(Marie Curie：A Life)，当然，还有雷德尼斯(Redniss)精彩的漫画小说。)。
根据官方假设：放射性是关于Marie SkłOdowska Curie和她的突破性科学成就的令人难以置信的真实故事，她发现了镭和钋，这些成就彻底改变了医学，最终永远改变了科学的面貌。玛丽是第一位获得诺贝尔奖的女性，也是历史上第一个两次获得这一殊荣的人。
这部电影以1934年开始，66岁的玛丽(派克饰)晕倒在她的实验室里，被迅速送往医院。当她被推着穿过走廊时，我们瞥见了这个女人的生活，然后在巴黎定居下来，当时她还是个年轻的女人，渴望用她的科学研究来证明自己。从那里开始，故事基本上是按时间顺序展开的。我们看到易怒的玛丽与物理学家加布里埃尔·利普曼(西蒙·罗素·比尔饰，佩妮·德雷德福饰)就她对实验室空间的不满而发生冲突；他的反应是将她完全驱逐出去。与皮埃尔·居里(萨姆·莱利，玛琳菲森饰)的一次偶然相遇导致了后者提出分享他自己的实验室。
皮埃尔被科学迷住了，也同样被这个女人的激情和鲁莽迷住了。他提议与她建立研究伙伴关系，让她使用他和他的兄弟雅克发明的一种新型静电计，这种静电计能够测量极低的电流-这正是检测空气中非常微弱的电流所需的，而空气中的非常微弱的电流是铀射线的证据。爱情绽放，两人举行了一场小型的民间婚礼。(玛丽的海军蓝婚纱在她的实验室工作中同样适用。)。
在乡村骑车度过蜜月后，这对情侣继续他们的实验，最终宣布发现了两种新元素：钋和镭。他们在学术同行面前发表了这一重大声明，玛丽宣布：我们在这里告诉你，你们从根本上误解了原子。正是玛丽创造了放射性一词，并提出了革命性的假设，即虽然大多数原子都是有限和稳定的，但有些原子不是有限和稳定的，而且在它们不稳定的情况下，它们会发出射线。
这部电影继续追踪玛丽的一生，从皮埃尔的悲惨死亡-他在过马路时被一辆马车撞倒-以及她与已婚物理学家(皮埃尔的前学生)保罗·朗之万(Anerin Barnard，敦刻尔克)的丑闻，一直到获得第二个诺贝尔奖(化学奖)。我们看到她18岁的女儿艾琳(Anya Taylor-Joy，Split，Glass饰)说服她在第一次世界大战期间帮助将便携式X射线设备带到法国战场，然后我们又回到居里夫人的死讯上，最后一张照片集中在1927年著名的索尔维会议(Solvay Conference)的照片上。玛丽·居里(Marie Curie)是这次世界上最著名的物理学家会议上唯一被拍到的女性，她在场讨论当时羽翼未丰的领域是什么。
对历史采取的大多数自由包括压缩时间线来简化叙事-这对于任何传记片来说都几乎是理所当然的。当威廉·伦琴发现X射线时，居里夫妇已经结婚六个月了(1901年获得了第一个诺贝尔物理学奖)。亨利·贝克勒尔在1896年初发表了他的洞察力，即铀盐发出的射线会使照相底片雾化。贝克勒尔的铀射线如此令玛丽着迷，以至于她把它作为自己研究的重点。
玛丽早年在波兰的大部分生活充其量只是在辐射期间短暂的闪回，包括玛丽的母亲在玛丽11岁时死于肺结核。与此同时，她与玛丽曾担任家庭教师的一个富裕家庭的儿子的一段年轻失败的恋情从未被提及过。这段恋情充其量只是一段短暂的闪回，其中包括玛丽的母亲在玛丽11岁时死于肺结核。与此同时，玛丽与一个富裕家庭的儿子的一段年轻失败的罗曼史从未被提及。
萨特拉皮不仅巧妙地编织了那个时代科学的细节，也巧妙地编织了它的艺术和更广泛的知识界的细节。举个例子：居里夫妇确实非常钦佩当时在巴黎风靡一时的美国现代舞先驱洛伊·富勒(Loie Fuller)。富勒也是剧院照明的先驱，使用不同颜色的灯来照亮她在表演时穿的丝绸面料。她著名的蛇形舞蹈于1897年被卢米埃兄弟拍摄成电影，这将启发泰勒·斯威夫特一个多世纪后的声誉巡演的编舞。
钦佩是相互的：富勒对居里夫妇的镭实验如此着迷，以至于她在1905年写信给他们，询问用镭制作服装的可能性(她并不知道供应是多么有限)。玛丽礼貌地建议不要这样做。富勒没有被吓倒，她在一件黑色纱布连衣裙上加入了荧光盐，她穿着这件连衣裙表演了她的Radium Dance，在黑暗的舞台上旋转时，她创造了一种闪烁的星星或幽灵般的灯光围绕着她的错觉。富勒引人注目的视觉效果在“放射性”中的几个场景中被重现。
在放射性环里的片刻都是假的。例如，皮埃尔着迷于唯心主义，他和玛丽参加了臭名昭著的意大利媒体尤萨皮亚·帕拉迪诺(Eusapia Palladino)的几次降神会，最终被揭露为骗子，这是真的。他们的兴趣纯粹是科学上的(皮埃尔的哥哥雅克是一个真正的信徒)，尽管正如电影所描绘的那样，玛丽远没有她的丈夫那么感兴趣。虽然降神会的几个方面显然是骗人的，但皮埃尔觉得可能有一些有趣的物理现象在起作用，因为有几个效应不太容易解释。在世界刚刚发现不可见的X射线和放射性的时候，他的假设并不是不合理的，那个时期的其他著名科学家也有皮埃尔的兴趣：威廉·克鲁克斯、让·佩林和朗之万，仅举几例。
但这不能成为场景的借口--在皮埃尔死后，朗之万结束了他们的恋情--在这场戏中，心烦意乱的玛丽寻找帕拉迪诺，却发现媒介已经死了。(有趣的事实：虽然朗之万回到了他的妻子身边，但玛丽的孙女玛丽·赫莱恩嫁给了他的孙子米歇尔。)。玛丽疯狂地敲门，然后倒在地上，反复恳求帕拉迪诺的前助手为她演绎皮埃尔的精神。这是对失去生命中的挚爱的过于人性化的回应吗？“。”当然了。这并不意味着这将是她人类对这一损失的反应，这也与派克对这个角色的解释不一致。
特别是，这些序列破坏了一个真正崇高的早期场景，在葬礼之前，玛丽站在皮埃尔敞开的棺材旁边。在陷入绝望的悲伤嚎叫之前，她看上去控制得很严。萨特拉皮让整件事在几分钟内上演，大部分时间近乎沉默，让我们看到派克脸上复杂的情绪，因为她正在为那宣泄的时刻而努力。
另一个小失误涉及玛丽和皮埃尔之间的打架。在这对夫妇获得诺贝尔奖后，他刚刚在斯德哥尔摩发表了传统的获奖感言。她指责他过于看重对同龄人的奉承，不带她一起去贬低她的贡献。玛丽坚称自己的头脑比他的好，并哀叹自己最大的弱点就是太爱他了。
居里夫妇可能有他们那份争论，玛丽经常因为得不到她觉得自己应得的职业尊重而感到沮丧。她还认为皮埃尔没有得到他应得的尊重。但是，正如所写的那样，这一幕对皮埃尔是一种伤害，他在妻子方面对那个时代的男人来说是非同寻常的。毕竟，是他坚持要把玛丽包括在奖项中，而且他们两人实际上是一起去了斯德哥尔摩-在颁奖两年后，因为他们的教学职责阻止了他们更早去斯德哥尔摩。
所有这些都不会减损派克细致入微的表演，这将这位著名的物理学家带入了生动的生活，即使电影其余部分的色调非常有限，偶尔的火花也不会完全点燃成一场大火。这位著名的物理学家将这位著名的物理学家带入了生动的生活，即使电影其余部分的色调非常有限，偶尔的火花也不会完全点燃。找出如何在不滑入给观众讲课的情况下直观地传达科学是科学传记片的一个挑战，但萨特拉皮巧妙地穿针引线，传达了主要概念，而不会太陷入细节的泥潭。
这部电影还很好地利用了玛丽喜欢随身携带小瓶镭和喜欢在晚上参观实验室的视觉效果，因为那些发光的管子看起来就像仙灯一样。她早期研究的所有论文，以及实验室书籍，甚至食谱，仍然被密封在铅盒里。她和皮埃尔都没有意识到放射性的危险；两人都时不时地被镭灼伤，经常得放射病。玛丽最终会死于再生障碍性贫血，这与她的暴露有关；她的女儿艾琳(Irène)因长期暴露在钚和X射线下而死于急性白血病。
不那么有效的是断断续续地闪现到未来，旨在展示玛丽的科学遗产：不仅是癌症的放射治疗，还有埃诺拉·盖伊(Enola Gay)在广岛和长崎投下的原子弹，20世纪50年代的核试验，以及1986年的切尔诺贝利灾难。诚然，所有的科学都建立在前人的基础上，但把原子弹和核电站灾难直接与居里的放射性研究联系起来是一种牵强；她在原子分裂改变世界的四年前去世。话虽如此，但我喜欢萨特拉皮在最后的死亡场景中使用闪影图像的方式，作为分隔过去、现在和未来的面纱落在玛丽的脑海里，她又一次与皮埃尔重聚了。
放射性可能不是每个人都喜欢的，而且它也不是完美的。但它承担了令人钦佩的艺术风险，而不是稳妥行事。仅凭这一点，这部电影就非常值得一看。它现在正在亚马逊Prime上流媒体播放。
note_en:
A resolute young woman in Paris in the 1890s sets the scientific world ablaze with her revolutionary discoveries in  Radioactive, a film about the life of Marie Curie, based on the 2010 graphic novel   Radioactive: Marie & Pierre Curie, A Tale of Love and Fallout, by Lauren Redniss. Director  Marjane Satrapi's film is part earnest biopic, part arthouse film, elevated by a luminous, intense, and riveting performance by Rosamund Pike ( Gone Girl) as Marie Curie.
  Satrapi is perhaps best known for her powerful autobiographical memoir,   Persepolis, depicting her childhood in Iran during the Islamic Revolution in graphic novel form, which she later adapted into  an animated film. So it's not surprising that she would admire Redniss' graphic novel about Marie Curie. Still, Satrapi admitted in an interview that she was initially reluctant to take on the project. "I was like, why the hell would you make another script about Marie Curie? There are already four of them,"  she told WWD. In the end, she became "obsessed" with making the film, which she views as being as much about the aftermath of Marie Curie's discoveries as her life and science.
 While Satrapi used the draft screenplay by Jack Thorne as the basis for her film, she also mined Marie's diaries and letters for material and met with the Curies' granddaughter, nuclear physicist  Hélène Langevin-Joliot, to learn more about this singular woman. "I really tried to portray her as a human being,"  Satrapi told WWD. "She's not perfect and she doesn't do everything right—but who does?" (The American Institute of Physics has long maintained a detailed online exhibit, " Marie Curie and The Science of Radioactivity," for those keen to delve into the details. I also highly recommend Susan Quinn's 1996 biography   Marie Curie: A Life and, of course, Redniss's wonderful graphic novel.)
 Per the official premise: " Radioactive is the incredible, true story of Marie Skłodowska Curie and her ground-breaking scientific achievements that revolutionized medicine with her discovery of radium and polonium, ultimately changing the face of science forever. Marie was the first female to win the Nobel Prize and the first person in history to win the esteemed award twice."
 The film opens in 1934 with a 66-year-old Marie (Pike) collapsing in her lab and being whisked off to the hospital. As she is wheeled down a corridor, we get glimpses of the woman's life, before settling on Marie as a young woman in Paris, keen to prove herself with her scientific research. From there, the story unfolds mostly chronologically. We see the prickly Marie clash with physicist  Gabriel Lippman (Simon Russell Beale,  Penny Dreadful)) over her frustrations with her laboratory space; his response is to evict her entirely. A chance encounter with Pierre Curie (Sam Riley,  Maleficent) leads to the latter offering to share his own laboratory.
     Pierre is fascinated by the science and equally entranced by the passion and temerity of the woman. He proposes a research partnership, offering her the use of a new type of electrometer he and his brother Jacques have invented, capable of measuring extremely low electrical currents—precisely what was required to detect the very faint currents in air that are evidence of uranium rays. Love blooms, and the two marry in a small civil ceremony. (Marie's navy blue wedding dress proves equally serviceable in her laboratory work.)
 After a honeymoon spent bicycling through the countryside, the lovers resume their experiments, eventually announcing the discovery of two new elements: polonium and radium. They make this momentous announcement before their academic peers, with Marie announcing, "We are here to tell you that you have fundamentally misunderstood the atom." It was Marie who coined the term radioactivity and proposed the revolutionary hypothesis that, while most atoms are "finite and stable," some are not, "and in their instability, they emit rays."
 The film continues to follow Marie's life, from Pierre's tragic death—he was run down by a horse-drawn cart while crossing the street—and her scandalous affair with married physicist (and Pierre's former student)  Paul Langevin (Aneurin Barnard,  Dunkirk), to winning a second Nobel Prize (in chemistry). We see her 18-year-old daughter, Irène (Anya Taylor-Joy,  Split, Glass), persuade her to help bring portable X-ray units onto French battlefields during World War I, before we come full circle to Curie's death, with one final shot centered on a photograph of the famous  1927 Solvay Conference. Marie Curie is the only woman pictured in this meeting of the world's most notable physicists, on hand to discuss what was then the fledgling field of quantum theory.
 Most of the liberties taken with the history involve condensing timelines to streamline the narrative—pretty much  de rigeur for any biopic. The Curies had already been married for six months when  Wilhelm Roentgen discovered X-rays (winning the very first Nobel Prize in physics in 1901).  Henri Becquerel published his insight that uranium salts emitted rays that would fog a photographic plate in early 1896. Becquerel's uranium rays so fascinated Marie that she made them the focus of her own research.
 Much of Marie's early life in Poland is, at best, briefly relayed in flashbacks during  Radioactive, including Marie's mother dying from tuberculosis when Marie was just 11. Meanwhile, a youthful, failed romance with the son of an affluent family with whom Marie served as governess is never mentioned at all.
 Satrapi has cleverly woven in details not just of the science of that age but also its art and broader intellectual circles. Case in point: it is true that the Curies greatly admired the American modern dance pioneer  Loie Fuller, who was all the rage in Paris at the time. Fuller was also a pioneer in theatrical lighting, using lights of different colors to illuminate the silk fabrics she wore during performances. Her famous "Serpentine Dance" was captured on film by the  Lumière brothers in 1897, and it would inspire choreography for Taylor Swift's  Reputation tour more than a century later.
 The admiration was mutual: Fuller was so captivated by the Curies' radium experiments that she wrote to them in 1905, asking about the possibility of making a costume out of radium (she was unaware of just how limited a supply was in existence). Marie politely advised against it. Undeterred, Fuller worked fluorescent salts into a black gauze dress that she wore to perform her " Radium Dance," creating the illusion of twinkling stars or ghostly lights surrounding her as she swirled on a darkened stage. Fuller's striking visuals are recreated to great effect in a couple of scenes in  Radioactive.
     A few moments in  Radioactive ring false. For instance, it's true that Pierre was fascinated by spiritualism and that he and Marie attended a few seances by the infamous Italian medium  Eusapia Palladino, who was ultimately revealed to be a fraud. Their interest was purely scientific (Pierre's brother Jacques was a true believer), although as the film depicts, Marie was far less intrigued than her husband. While several aspects of the seances were clearly tricks, Pierre felt there might be interesting physics phenomena at play for a couple of effects not as easily explained. At a time when the world had just discovered invisible X-rays and radioactivity, his hypothesis wasn't unreasonable, and other notable scientists of the period shared Pierre's interest: William Crookes, Jean Perrin, and Langevin, to name a few.
 But that's no excuse for a scene—following Pierre's death and Langevin terminating their affair—in which a distraught Marie seeks out Palladino only to find the medium has died. (Fun fact: although Langevin returned to his wife, Marie's granddaughter Hélène married his grandson Michel.) Marie frantically pounds on the door before collapsing to the ground, begging Palladino's former assistant repeatedly to produce Pierre's spirit for her. Is this an all-too-human response to losing the love of one's life? Of course. That doesn't mean this would have been  her human response to the loss, and it's inconsistent with Pike's interpretation of the character.
 In particular, these sequences undermine a truly sublime earlier scene in which Marie stands beside Pierre's open casket, prior to the funeral. She's the picture of tight control before breaking down into a despairing howl of sorrow. Satrapi lets the whole thing play out over several minutes, much of it in near-silence, letting us see the complicated emotions playing across Pike's face as she builds to that moment of catharsis.
 Another minor misstep involves a fight between Marie and Pierre. He's just returned from giving the traditional acceptance speech in Stockholm after the couple has won the Nobel Prize. She accuses him of placing too much value on the adulation of his peers and of trivializing her contribution by not taking her with him. Marie insists she has a finer mind than his and laments that her biggest weakness is that she loves him so much.
  The Curies likely had their share of arguments, and Marie was often frustrated with not receiving the professional respect she felt she deserved. She also thought Pierre didn't get the respect he deserved. But this scene, as written, does a disservice to Pierre, who was extraordinary for a man of that era with regard to his wife. He was the one who insisted Marie be included in the award, after all, and the two of them actually did travel to Stockholm together—two years after the prize was awarded, since their teaching commitments prevented then from going sooner.
 None of this detracts from Pike's tautly nuanced performance, which brings the famous physicist to vivid life, even if the rest of the film is so tonally constrained that the occasional sparks never quite ignite into a fire. Figuring out how to convey the science visually without slipping into lecturing the audience is a challenge with scientific biopics, but Satrapi deftly threads that needle and conveys the main concepts without getting too bogged down in the details.
 The film also makes good visual use of how Marie liked to carry around vials of radium and enjoyed visiting the lab at night because "the glowing tubes looked like fairy lights." All her papers from her early research, along with laboratory books and even her cookbooks, are still kept sealed in lead boxes. Neither she nor Pierre realized the dangers of radioactivity; both suffered occasional radium burns and regular bouts of radiation sickness. Marie would eventually die of aplastic anemia, linked to that exposure; her daughter Irène died of acute leukemia from her prolonged exposure to polonium and X-rays.
 Less effective are the intermittent flash forwards into the future, intended to show Marie's scientific legacy: not just radiation therapy for cancer, but also the  atomic bombs dropped on Hiroshima and Nagasaki by the   Enola Gay, the  nuclear tests of the 1950s, and the  1986 Chernobyl disaster, for instance. Granted, all science builds on what came before, but it's a stretch to  directly tie the atomic bomb and nuclear power plant disasters to Curie's research on radioactivity; she died four years before the splitting of the atom changed the world. That said, I loved how Satrapi used that flash-forward imagery in the final death scene, as the veils that separate past, present, and future drop in Marie's mind, and she is "reunited" once more with Pierre.
 Radioactive might not be to everyone's taste, and it's not perfect. But it takes admirable artistic risks rather than playing it safe. That alone makes the film well worth watching. It's now streaming on Amazon Prime.

为了帮助打击这一问题，总部位于伦敦的初创公司ComplyAdvantage今天宣布，将向其提供5000万美元的增长轮资金，以扩大其覆盖范围和运营范围。ComplyAdvantage已经建立了一个人工智能平台和更广泛的数据库，包含约1000万个实体，以帮助识别和跟踪那些参与金融犯罪的人。
具体地说，未来的计划将是将资金用于招聘，投资于它用来检测实体并绘制它们之间关系的工具，并吸引更多的客户。
创始人兼首席执行官查尔斯·德林波尔(Charles Delingpol)在接受采访时表示：“我们一直专注于更细粒度的分析，并能够在我们的数据库中扩展到数亿次搜索。”“下一阶段将更多地围绕人脉网络，加强勤奋工作。”该公司目前约有250名员工，主要分布在英国和罗马尼亚。
C系列由安大略省教师养老金计划委员会(安大略省教师养老金计划委员会)领导，该委员会是加拿大的一个庞大的养老金计划(1550亿美元)，以多产的成长期科技投资者而闻名。之前的支持者Balderton和Index也在融资中。到目前为止，该公司已经筹集了8800万美元，虽然没有披露估值，但根据PitchBook的数据，在某种背景下，它上一轮的估值约为1.41亿美元。
今天，ComplyAdvantage拥有500多个客户，主要是金融机构，他们使用它来满足法规遵从性要求，并减少自己的风险敞口和风险，提供一些自动化服务来补充(并有可能取代)他们进行的一些手动检查，以证明您就是您所说的那个人。
它与其他出于自身目的追踪欺诈的集团的业务也在不断增长，比如试图阻止欺诈性索赔的保险公司和政府实体。它也有一些合作伙伴访问它的数据库，并将其作为自己解决方案的一部分(Quantexa上周宣布了自己的一轮大笔融资，就是这些许可合作伙伴之一)。
德林波尔说：“更广泛的身份空间中的许多公司都是由我们的数据提供动力的，即使他们不披露这些数据。”
该公司最初专注于帮助银行满足欺诈检测方面的监管合规要求的过程，方法是在开户之前摄取和分析客户提供的文件，启动与新实体的更大规模交易等。近年来，随着ComplyAdvantage的数据库变得更深，这一目标变得更加有针对性。
今天，这项业务的核心是一个中央数据库，其中包括已知的洗钱者、人贩子、恐怖分子、毒枭和其他利用金融轨道进行非法操作并从中获利的人。
德林波尔说，它是通过“自动摄取数万个数据点的方式形成的，这些数据点来自网站、国家预警名单、相关公司的链接实时数据库，以及其他各种应用程序。”这个中央数据库仍在增长，德林波尔认为，为了获得最准确的图像，它运行到一个更高的数字并不是不切实际的。
“虽然我们今天有1000万，但我们希望有一天能覆盖每一家公司和每一个人。我们认为正确的数字是80亿“--也就是世界人口。“有了这个更大的数据库，我们还可以破获其他类型的犯罪。”
这家初创公司已经有了与政府机构直接联系的渠道，代表他们的客户直接向他们报告联系和发现。需要明确的是，尽管欧洲现在有强有力的数据保护措施，当人们与非法活动有关时，这会将他们列入取代非法活动的名单。当有人被怀疑并被告知当局时，这些信息是保密的。
虽然所有机构都将继续有专门的人员团队致力于风险分析和活动调查，但这里的想法是用更多的数据来加强这些工作，帮助这些调查人员处理当今世界上更大规模的数据。
“如果没有数据科学和机器学习的应用，在全球发生的数十亿交易中侦测金融犯罪几乎是不可能的。Index Ventures的合伙人简·哈默在一份声明中说：“正是这种方法使ComplyAdvantage成为该领域的领先者，并成为那些寻求将仍然经常是手动或不充分的流程自动化的组织的首选合作伙伴。
更长期的机会是通过利用ComplyAdvantage已经出现的可能与其他垂直市场相关的信息来扩大ComplyAdvantage的客户基础。
德林波尔说，保险是一个关键的例子。“我们已经看到有人拖欠贷款，然后提出保险索赔，”他说。“我们把信贷、欺诈和所有权数据放在一起看。”
当然，这不仅使该公司与其他构建信用数据库的公司展开了激烈的竞争，也使该公司与那些构建强大的人工智能平台的公司展开了激烈的竞争，这些平台利用数据来更深入地洞察看似不同的数字行动，并代表客户构建更好的活动图景。这不仅包括Quantexa这样的合作伙伴，还包括Palantir这样的其他合作伙伴。
德林波尔说，这里的优势在于ComplyAdvantage数据库的巨大规模，以及它对金融犯罪的非常具体的关注，以及那些出于自身业务健康和监管原因需要对其进行监管的公司的处境。正是这种关注吸引了投资。
安大略省教师协会(TIP)高级总经理奥利维亚·斯蒂德曼(Olivia Steedman)说：“ComplyAdvantage为打击金融犯罪和跟上不断发展的监管格局提供关键任务的技术解决方案。随着其强大的技术平台改变其客户的合规和风险管理流程，该公司处于有利地位，可以继续快速增长。“
